message:
feat(core,parser,mvcc): add FEC/tokenize metrics, harden repair validation, improve bench fidelity

Add SnapshotFecMetrics with atomic counters for encoded pages, sidecar
bytes, and encode operations as a global singleton. Add bounds validation
in compute_raptorq_repair_symbols() rejecting source_pages.len() !=
meta.group_size with DatabaseCorrupt, and out-of-group-range validation
in detect_and_repair_page() returning Unrecoverable early for pages
outside [start_pgno, start_pgno + group_size).

Instrument the parser lexer with tracing spans at TRACE level and
per-token debug events. Add TokenizeDurationSecondsHistogram (6 latency
buckets from <=100us to >5ms) and token counter as global atomic metrics
with snapshot/reset support. Gate character-level TRACE logging behind a
one-time tracing::enabled! check to avoid hot-path overhead. Add tracing
as a workspace dependency.

Improve MVCC benchmarks by checksumming visible rows via byte-level
wrapping add instead of black_boxing row counts, ensuring the compiler
cannot elide actual page reads. Add sleep intervals in lifecycle stress
tests for timing measurement stability.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-14m.4","title":"TEST: WAL crash recovery, parallel ordering, torn-write detection","description":"Test bead: WAL durability & recovery. (1) Crash injection: kill process at every WAL write point (frame write, index update, checkpoint), verify recovery correctness. Use deterministic fault injection (franken_fault). (2) Parallel WAL ordering: verify commit order matches epoch order under concurrent writers. (3) Torn write: simulate partial page writes, verify detection and recovery. (4) Checkpoint stress: concurrent reads during checkpoint, verify no visible corruption. (5) WAL growth bounds: verify checkpoint prevents unbounded growth. Acceptance: zero data loss in 10K crash-recovery cycles, correct ordering in parallel WAL.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Zero data loss in 10K crash-recovery cycles. 2. Parallel WAL ordering matches epoch order. 3. Torn writes detected and recovered. 4. Checkpoint concurrent with reads: no corruption.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:01:03.418672823Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T18:13:58.702434558Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["frankensqlite","test","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14m.4","depends_on_id":"bd-14m","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14m.4","depends_on_id":"bd-14m.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-14vp7","title":"Vectorized Execution + Morsel-Driven Parallelism (§8.2)","description":"Replace row-at-a-time VDBE interpretation with vectorized batch processing and morsel-driven parallel execution. This is scored 10.0 in the opportunity matrix — the single largest known performance uplift available.

## Background
SQLite's VDBE processes one row at a time with significant per-row interpretation overhead (opcode dispatch, type checking, memory allocation). Modern analytical engines (DuckDB, HyPer, MonetDB/X100) process data in columnar batches of 1024-4096 rows, amortizing interpretation overhead and enabling SIMD vectorization.

## Mathematical Foundation
Boncz et al. 2005 (MonetDB/X100) showed that vectorized execution achieves near-hardware-limit throughput by keeping data in CPU caches. Leis et al. 2014 (morsel-driven parallelism) showed that splitting work into cache-sized 'morsels' and dispatching to worker threads achieves near-linear scaling with cores while maintaining low scheduling overhead.

## Key Papers
- Boncz, Zukowski, Nes 2005: 'MonetDB/X100: Hyper-Pipelining Query Execution'
- Leis, Boncz, Kemper, Neumann 2014: 'Morsel-Driven Parallelism: A NUMA-Aware Query Evaluation Framework'
- Kersten, Leis, Kemper, Neumann, Pavlo 2018: 'Everything You Always Wanted to Know About Compiled and Vectorized Query Engines'

## Implementation Plan
1. Define columnar batch format (Arrow-compatible): arrays of typed values, validity bitmaps for NULLs
2. Vectorized operators: scan, filter, project, hash-join, aggregation, sort
3. SIMD primitives for filter evaluation, hash computation, comparison
4. Morsel dispatcher: partition table scans into morsels, assign to worker threads via work-stealing
5. Pipeline breakers (hash-join build, sort) use parallel partitioned algorithms

## Expected Impact
- 5-20x throughput improvement on analytical queries (scan-heavy, filter-heavy)
- Near-linear scaling with CPU cores for parallel queries
- Reduced memory bandwidth pressure through columnar layout

## Connection to Other Features
- Leapfrog Triejoin can emit vectorized batches for downstream operators
- SSI read-set tracking must handle batch-level granularity
- Buffer pool (S3-FIFO) feeds vectorized scan with page-aligned reads
- EBR epoch management aligns with morsel boundaries

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Columnar batch format defined with Arrow compatibility
- [ ] All core operators vectorized (scan, filter, join, agg, sort)
- [ ] SIMD acceleration for filter and hash operations
- [ ] Morsel-driven parallel dispatcher with work-stealing
- [ ] TPC-H queries run end-to-end through vectorized engine
- [ ] Benchmark: 5x+ improvement over interpreter on TPC-H SF1","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:17:38.265137567Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:17:38.265137567Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","execution-engine","performance"]}
 {"id":"bd-14vp7.1","title":"Design vectorized batch format (columnar, 1024-row batches)","description":"Design the core columnar batch format for vectorized execution. Each batch contains 1024 rows (tunable) stored in columnar layout: one array per column. Design considerations:

1. Column types: integers (i8/i16/i32/i64), floats (f32/f64), blobs, text (offset+data), NULL bitmap
2. Memory layout: aligned arrays for SIMD (32-byte or 64-byte alignment)
3. Validity bitmap: one bit per row per column for NULL tracking
4. Selection vector: indices of 'active' rows (for filter pushdown without copying)
5. Ownership: arena-allocated per-batch, freed as a unit
6. Arrow compatibility: layout should be zero-copy convertible to/from Arrow arrays

The batch format is the fundamental data exchange unit between all vectorized operators. Getting this right is critical — it determines SIMD compatibility, cache behavior, and memory efficiency.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Batch format specification document
- [ ] Rust type definitions for Batch, Column, SelectionVector
- [ ] Construction from row-oriented input (for compatibility)
- [ ] Zero-copy Arrow conversion (both directions)
- [ ] Micro-benchmark: batch construction throughput
- [ ] Memory alignment verified for SIMD operations","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:17:50.057650596Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:49:24.153594148Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T09:49:24.153572818Z","close_reason":"Implemented vectorized batch format module, row->batch builder, Arrow-compatible zero-copy interchange, alignment verification, benchmark, and spec doc","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","design","execution-engine"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.1","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:22:58Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-3bw.4","title":"Proofs of Retrievability (PoR) for storage audit","description":"Proofs of Retrievability (§11.11): Cryptographic proof that stored data has not been lost or corrupted.  {"id":"bd-3bw.5","title":"TEST: RaptorQ repair correctness, symbol efficiency, PoR audit","description":"Test bead: RaptorQ self-healing correctness. (1) Bit-flip injection: corrupt random bytes in WAL/snapshot, verify transparent repair. (2) Symbol efficiency: verify decode succeeds with overhead < 2% above information-theoretic minimum. (3) Async encoding latency: verify repair symbol generation does not increase write latency by > 5%. (4) PoR audit: verify proofs detect 1-bit corruption. (5) End-to-end: corrupt 5% of pages, verify full database recovery. Acceptance: 100% repair rate for corruption up to configured redundancy, zero false PoR failures.","acceptance_criteria":"1. 100% repair rate up to configured redundancy. 2. Decode overhead < 2% above minimum. 3. Write latency increase < 5% from async encoding. 4. Zero false PoR audit failures.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:01:28.089352709Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T18:13:58.754769969Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["frankensqlite","raptorq","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3bw.5","depends_on_id":"bd-3bw","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3bw.5","depends_on_id":"bd-3bw.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-3bw.6","title":"Local Reconstruction Codes for distributed repair","description":"Local Reconstruction Codes (§1.4): For distributed FrankenSQLite, LRC enables local repair without full quorum. Erasure coding with local+global parities. Reduces repair I/O by 10-50x vs standard RS codes. Tracing: span 'lrc_repair' with fields locality_group, repair_type(local/global), symbols_read. Code/constants delivered:

  - added required/replayability constants: `LOG_SCHEMA_MIN_SUPPORTED_VERSION`, `REQUIRED_EVENT_FIELDS`, `REPLAYABILITY_KEYS`
  - added typed field contract metadata (`FieldValueType`, value constraints, semantics)
  - added versioning/compat policy API (`SchemaVersion`, `VersionTransition`, `ToolingCompatibility`, `build_schema_compatibility_policy`, `classify_version_transition`, `evaluate_tooling_compatibility`)
  - added markdown contract renderer + canonical examples + contract tests

Schema contract document:
- `docs/e2e_log_schema_contract.md`

E2E verification script updates:
- `scripts/verify_e2e_log_schema.sh`
  - validates contract doc presence/alignment
  - emits `contract_doc_hash` and contract status in JSON mode
  - tags `related_bead_ids: ["bd-mblr.5.3.1"]`

Verification evidence:
1) Unit/contract tests
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target_silverbadger_5_3_1 cargo test -p fsqlite-harness --lib e2e_log_schema -- --nocapture`
- Result: 17 passed, 0 failed

2) E2E script
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target_silverbadger_5_3_1 bash scripts/verify_e2e_log_schema.sh --json`
- Result: pass

3) Structured artifact
- `test-results/bd-mblr.5.3.1-schema-verify.json`
- sha256: `4a5d213521cac94cd89cce28870cf14768abced741f0c3886421ee1c296c6494`

Quality gates (workspace):
- `cargo check --all-targets` passed (with existing warnings)
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` fails on broad pre-existing lint backlog outside this slice
- `cargo fmt --all -- --check` fails due unrelated existing formatting drift in many files/workspaces

- e2e/bd_sxm2_compliance.sh

What was implemented:

- Added deterministic run-scoped report paths and JSON output summaries with report hashes.
- Preserved existing gate semantics (fail/pass behavior) while normalizing event shape.
